calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jul-08-2025 Vacated

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify Defendant Doe 1'S Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records; Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-28-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF L. B.'s Motion To Quash Or Modify Defendant Doe 1's Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records; Request For Sanctions.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff L.B.'s motion to quash or modify a subpoena issued by defendant Doe 1 and for sanctions is granted in part and denied in part.

L.B. alleges in her complaint that she was a childhood victim of recurring sexual assaults perpetrated by Doe 2, a pastor employed by Doe 1. In discovery responses, she contends that she suffered injuries from the assaults that include "trauma, hypervigilance, anger, feelings of mistrust, humiliation and shame, flashbacks, depression, frustration, and sadness." (Chau Dec. Ex. C.) Doe 1 has served a subpoena for production of business records on Tom Waddell Urban Health Center, a clinic where L.B. has received treatment that offers physical and mental health services, seeking essentially all records in Tom Waddell's possession relating to L.B. (Caigoy Dec. Ex. A.)

By reaching L.B.'s entire medical history, Doe 1's subpoena implicates areas of significant constitutionally protected privacy interests. "Privacy is an inalienable right under the California Constitution. It is well established that the right to privacy extends to medical records which may contain matters of great sensitivity going to the core of the concerns for the privacy of information about an individual." (Grafilo v. Soorani (2019) 41 Cal.App.5th 497, 507 [internal quotation marks and citations omitted].) Here, L.B. has put at issue only her mental and emotional state and not her entire medical history. Doe 1 argues otherwise based on the Albucher Declaration, which offers a psychiatrist's opinion that it is "plausible" that L.B.'s current psychological symptoms were caused by events in her life other than the alleged sexual assaults, and review of her complete medical record would inform that alternative causation. The court finds this too slender a reed to outweigh L.B.'s privacy right and support production of the complete medical record. (See Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 855-857.) Doe 1 has less invasive methods to determine whether other events in L.B.'s life could have led to her current symptoms, including targeted interrogatories on this topic or deposition questions to L.B.

L.B. concedes, however, that she has put her mental state at issue. Discovery of business records that relate to the mental and emotional conditions that she has identified is appropriate here. The court grants the motion to quash only in part and narrows the categories of requested documents as follows for the period 1980 to the present:

All documents referencing or relating to any medical and/or counseling treatment given to L.B. as a result of her alleged emotional distress;

All documents evidencing billing(s) and/or payment(s) for any medical and/or counseling treatment given to L.B. as a result of her alleged emotional distress;

All treatment notes, patient notes, billing records, payment records, and other documents regarding mental, emotional, or behavioral health treatment provided to L.B.

The court further orders Defendants to safeguard the confidence of any records produced in response to the subpoena; to use them only in connection with this litigation; to refrain from sharing them outside of Defendant's counsel's firm except in connection with this litigation to Doe 1 and Doe 2 and to retained experts who agree to be bound by this protective order; and to destroy them or return them to L.B. within 90 days of the final conclusion of this litigation.

Both parties seek sanctions. The court finds both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. =(301/CVA)

PLAINTIFF L. B. AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO QUASH OR MODIFY DEFENDANT DOE 1'S DEPOSITION SUBPOENA FOR PRODUCTION OF BUSINESS RECORDS; REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (ADDED FOR POSTING OF TENTATIVE RULING)
Off Calendar Apr-28-2025 Off Calendar

Notice Of And Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories, Set Two
Off Calendar Mar-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:
DEFENDANT DOE 1's Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories, Set Two.

The court grants the motion to compel further response to Form Interrogatories, Set Two, in part. The court does not find persuasive the argument that the court should use meet and confer letters to test the truthfulness of an interrogatory response. The fact that defendant believes additional documents or information should be available does not make it so. Plaintiff has verified her responses and is thereby bound to them.

However, the court finds that Plaintiff's initial verified form interrogatory responses were not sufficiently complete or straightforward. Plaintiff has cured the deficiencies in part by straightforward, verified amended responses, served after the filing of the motion, that make clear she does not remember the names or locations of any treatment provider. But her initial verified responses stated that she lost her job as a security guard as a result of the alleged sexual abuse. On this account, her previous employer is a person with knowledge of her injuries. Plaintiff shall provide the name and contact information of this previous employer in an amended response due within 10 days of entry of this order.

Defendant seeks sanctions. The court finds that Plaintiff's initial responses, and her failure to amend during meet and confer efforts, were not substantially justified. (See Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390, 411 (court may award sanctions even where a party amends while motion to compel is pending].) The court reduces the requested sanctions amount in light of Defendant's modest success. Plaintiff shall pay $100 in sanctions to Defendant through counsel within 10 days of notice of entry of this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

Notice Of And Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents, Set One
Off Calendar Mar-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DOE 1's Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents, Set One.

The court denies the motion to compel further responses to request for production of documents as untimely. In answers served on July 2, 2024, Plaintiff asserted, in verified responses, that she did not have any responsive documents. Defendant did not move to compel further responses within the 45 days provided. The discovery deadline to move to compel is jurisdictional, and the court lacks authority to order further responses at this point. (Weinstein v. Blumberg (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 316, 322, fn. 3 ["Prejudice is not required; discovery deadlines are mandatory and we have treated them as jurisdictional (Sexton v. Superior Court (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1410), even though a trial court may grant relief from deadlines to file motions to compel. Where a party does not obtain trial court relief from the statutory deadline, 'failure to move for further answers within the statutory time forecloses further relief ....' (O'Brien v. Superior Court (1965) 233 Cal.App.2d 388, 391.)"].)

Because Plaintiff's responses were straightforward and verified representations of her lack of documents, it is not clear what relief the court could order even if the motion were timely.

Defendant has not prevailed and is not entitled to sanctions. Plaintiff would be entitled to sanctions because Defendant's position is without substantial justification, but Plaintiff has not sought sanctions. The court does not award sanctions.

Proposed order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
